class_schedule.topic: Foundations of Visual Design and Layout Systems
learning_objectives: Understand the principles of visual hierarchy in digital media
Identify and apply grid systems for structured layouts
Analyze effective composition in professional design work
Apply Gestalt principles to organize visual information
readings: Making and Breaking the Grid by Timothy Samara, Ch. 1-2
The Elements of Typographic Style by Robert Bringhurst, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Digital Design Principles. This course builds on your foundation in digital art to develop professional design skills used across branding, UI, motion graphics, and interactive media. Design is not decoration -- it is the systematic organization of visual information to communicate meaning, guide attention, and create emotional responses. Every pixel on screen is a design decision, whether intentional or accidental. Intentional design creates clear, compelling, and accessible experiences. Accidental design creates confusion, frustration, and abandonment. Over fifteen weeks, we will master the core principles that separate professional design from amateur effort: grid systems that create structural integrity, typography that communicates hierarchy and personality, color theory that ensures accessibility and emotional resonance, motion design that provides feedback and delight, responsive layouts that adapt across devices, and design systems that scale across products and teams. We begin with the foundation of all visual design: the grid. Grids are not constraints -- they are the invisible architecture that gives your design rhythm, consistency, and order. From the 12-column web grid to the modular grids of editorial design, understanding grid systems is the single most important structural skill for digital designers.
key_concepts: Grid systems: column grids (12-column standard), modular grids (rows + columns), hierarchical grids (organic zones)
Visual hierarchy: size, color, contrast, proximity, alignment, whitespace as tools for guiding viewer attention
Gestalt principles: proximity (grouped elements relate), similarity (matching elements relate), closure (completing incomplete shapes), continuity (following lines)
Whitespace as design element: breathing room that increases comprehension, perceived quality, and visual focus
Responsive grid adaptation: how grid structures collapse from 12 columns (desktop) to 8 (tablet) to 4 (mobile)
Baseline grid: aligning text across columns to a consistent vertical rhythm for professional typographic appearance
Margin and gutter specifications: defining the empty spaces between and around content areas
Composition rules: rule of thirds, golden ratio, focal point placement, and when to break rules intentionally
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of grid construction in Figma using a 12-column layout system applied to a portfolio landing page, showing column math, gutter configuration, and content placement

review.value: Column, modular, and hierarchical grids provide underlying structure that organizes content and creates visual rhythm. The 12-column grid is standard because 12 divides evenly by 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 12.
The arrangement of elements to guide the viewer's eye in a deliberate sequence from most to least important. Achieved through size, color, contrast, proximity, and whitespace.
Elements placed close together are perceived as belonging to the same group. This principle governs how users understand relationships between labels, inputs, and buttons without explicit borders.
Empty space is not wasted space. Strategic whitespace increases comprehension by 20%, improves perceived quality, and directs attention to content that matters.

class_schedule.topic: Typography and Color Theory for Screens
learning_objectives: Select and pair typefaces for readability and brand expression
Apply color theory to create accessible digital palettes
Understand typographic scale and its relationship to visual hierarchy
Build cohesive color systems with semantic meaning
readings: Thinking with Type by Ellen Lupton, Ch. 2-3
Interaction of Color by Josef Albers, Plates I-VII

lecture_notes.introduction: Typography and color are the two most powerful tools a digital designer wields. Typography communicates both content (the words) and character (the personality) simultaneously. A healthcare app set in Comic Sans communicates something very different from the same app set in Inter. Color creates emotional responses, directs attention, communicates meaning (red for error, green for success), and can include or exclude users depending on contrast choices. Today we learn to use both intentionally for clarity, emotion, and accessibility. We build a type scale using mathematical ratios, pair fonts that complement rather than compete, construct a color system that works for both light and dark themes, and verify that every color combination meets WCAG accessibility standards. These are not aesthetic exercises -- they are engineering decisions that directly affect whether your design is usable by all people or only some.
key_concepts: Type classification: serif (traditional, authoritative), sans-serif (modern, clean), monospace (technical, code), display (decorative, limited use)
Typographic scale: mathematically derived font sizes using ratios (1.25 minor third, 1.333 perfect fourth, 1.5 perfect fifth) for harmonious hierarchy
Type pairing: contrast principle (pair a serif with a sans-serif), shared proportions, limiting to 2-3 typefaces maximum
Color models: RGB (additive, screens), HSL (intuitive adjustment of hue, saturation, lightness), HEX (web standard notation)
Color accessibility: WCAG 2.1 contrast ratios (AA: 4.5:1 normal text, 3:1 large text; AAA: 7:1 normal text)
Building a color palette: primary (brand), secondary (accent), neutral (text, backgrounds), semantic (success, warning, error, info)
Color psychology: blue (trust, calm), red (urgency, danger), green (growth, success), yellow (caution, warmth) -- cultural variations apply
Line height and letter spacing: optimal body text line height is 1.4-1.6x font size; heading line height is 1.1-1.3x
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a type scale and color system in Figma using design tokens, then testing every color combination with WebAIM contrast checker to ensure AA compliance
setup_requirements: Figma with design token plugin installed
Google Fonts access for typeface exploration
Color palette generator tool (Coolors or similar)

review.value: A set of harmonious font sizes derived from a consistent ratio, creating visual rhythm and clear hierarchy. A 1.25 scale from 16px base produces: 16, 20, 25, 31, 39, 49.
WCAG 2.1 Level AA requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text and 3:1 for large text (18pt+). This is a legal requirement in many jurisdictions.
Colors that carry specific meaning: red for errors/destructive actions, green for success/confirmation, yellow for warnings, blue for information. Must maintain meaning across themes.

Pair typefaces with contrasting styles but compatible proportions. A geometric sans-serif heading (Montserrat) with a humanist serif body (Merriweather) creates visual interest while maintaining readability.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between logo, brand identity, and brand system
Construct a brand style guide with defined usage rules
Apply brand consistency across multiple digital touchpoints
Design marks that work across scales from favicon to billboard
readings: Designing Brand Identity by Alina Wheeler, Ch. 1-3
Logo Design Love by David Airey, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: A brand is far more than a logo. It is the complete system of visual and verbal elements that creates recognition, communicates values, and builds trust across every interaction a user has with your product. Today we explore how identity systems maintain consistency and communicate personality across platforms, contexts, and scales. A strong brand system allows a team of 50 designers to produce work that looks like it came from one mind. A weak brand system produces visual chaos that confuses users and erodes trust. We will build a mini brand guide in Figma covering the essential elements: logo construction and usage rules, color palette with accessibility verification, typography specification, spacing and layout rules, and correct versus incorrect usage examples. This exercise teaches you both the creative skills of identity design and the documentation skills that make your design decisions scalable.
key_concepts: Brand anatomy: logo/wordmark/icon, tagline, voice and tone, visual language (illustration style, photography direction)
Style guide structure: logo usage (clear space, minimum size, color variants), color palette, typography, imagery, spacing rules
Brand applications: web, mobile, print, social media, merchandise -- each platform with specific adaptation rules
Scalability: marks that work at 16px favicon, 44px app icon, social avatar, and billboard scale without losing recognition
Brand versioning: evolving a brand over time while maintaining recognition (responsive logos for different contexts)
Logo construction: using geometric forms, golden ratio, and visual correction for optically balanced marks
Brand voice: the personality expressed through writing style, from formal (financial) to playful (gaming) to authoritative (news)
Design debt: what happens when brand guidelines are ignored -- visual inconsistency that compounds over time
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a mini brand guide in Figma: constructing a logo from geometric forms, defining color tokens, specifying clear space rules, and documenting correct/incorrect usage

review.value: A cohesive set of visual and verbal elements governed by usage rules that ensure consistent recognition across all touchpoints. More than aesthetics -- it is a communication framework.
The minimum protected area around a logo where no other elements may intrude, typically defined as a multiple of a logo unit (height of the logomark or x-height of the wordmark).
Logo variants designed for different contexts: full lockup for large placements, icon-only for small contexts (app icons, favicons), and simplified versions for embroidery or engraving.
Like technical debt, brand debt accumulates when guidelines are ignored. Each inconsistent application makes the brand slightly less recognizable until the identity feels fragmented.

learning_objectives: Apply interface design patterns for common interaction scenarios
Design forms, navigation systems, and content layouts following established conventions
Create wireframes that communicate information architecture and user flow
Balance convention (user expectation) with innovation (brand differentiation) in UI design
readings: Don't Make Me Think by Steve Krug, Ch. 1-5
Material Design Guidelines (Google), Foundations section

lecture_notes.introduction: User interface design is where visual design meets human behavior. Every button, form field, navigation element, and content card is a designed interaction that either helps or hinders the user's goal. Good UI design is invisible -- users accomplish their tasks without thinking about the interface. Bad UI design is conspicuous -- users struggle, make errors, and blame themselves for what is actually a design failure. Today we learn the fundamental patterns of UI design: how to structure navigation that users can understand without instruction, how to design forms that minimize errors and frustration, how to organize content with cards, lists, and grids that match user mental models, and how to use established conventions while creating distinctive brand experiences. We study the major design systems (Material Design, Human Interface Guidelines, Fluent Design) not to copy them but to understand the research and reasoning behind their pattern recommendations. These systems represent thousands of hours of user testing distilled into reusable guidelines.
key_concepts: Navigation patterns: top nav (web), tab bar (mobile), side drawer (complex apps), breadcrumbs (deep hierarchy)
Form design: label placement (top-aligned fastest), error handling (inline validation), progressive disclosure, smart defaults
Card UI pattern: self-contained content units with image, title, description, and action -- the building block of modern interfaces
Content layout patterns: F-pattern (text-heavy), Z-pattern (marketing), hub-and-spoke (dashboard), master-detail (list + detail)
Design systems as references: Material Design (Google), Human Interface Guidelines (Apple), Fluent Design (Microsoft)
Affordance and signifiers: making interactive elements look interactive through visual cues (depth, color, cursor changes)
Error prevention vs error recovery: designing to prevent mistakes is better than designing good error messages
Loading states and skeleton screens: maintaining user confidence during data fetching with progressive content revelation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Wireframing a multi-page web application in Figma: creating low-fidelity wireframes for navigation, content pages, forms, and error states following established UI patterns

review.value: The visual properties of an element that suggest how it should be used. A raised button affords pressing. An underlined blue text affords clicking. Good affordances reduce the need for instructions.

Eye-tracking research shows users scan text-heavy pages in an F-shape: horizontal across the top, shorter horizontal scan below, then vertical down the left side. Place key information along this path.
Validating form fields as the user completes them rather than after submission. Reduces error correction effort by 22% and form completion time by 42% in research studies.
A loading state that shows the layout structure with placeholder shapes before content loads. Perceived as 50% faster than spinner-only loading states in user perception studies.

learning_objectives: Apply animation principles to UI motion design
Design purposeful micro-interactions that enhance usability
Specify motion parameters for developer handoff
Distinguish between functional motion (usability) and decorative motion (delight)
readings: The Illusion of Life by Thomas and Johnston, Ch. 3
Material Design Motion Guidelines (Google)

lecture_notes.introduction: Motion communicates relationships, provides feedback, and guides attention. When done well, it feels invisible -- users do not notice the animation, they just feel that the interface is responsive and intuitive. When done poorly, it frustrates users with unnecessary delays, distracting movements, or motion sickness. Every animation in a digital interface should serve one of four purposes: providing feedback (confirming an action was received), revealing relationships (showing where a new element came from), guiding attention (directing the eye to important changes), or expressing personality (creating emotional connection through movement character). Today we learn to design motion with intention: choosing appropriate durations (100-500ms for most UI), selecting easing curves that feel natural, choreographing staggered animations for lists, and specifying motion parameters precisely enough for developers to implement. We prototype micro-interactions in Figma and discuss the annotation systems that bridge the design-development gap for motion specifications.
key_concepts: Animation principles for UI: easing (natural acceleration), anticipation (preparation before action), follow-through (settling after action), staging (directing attention)
Micro-interaction anatomy: trigger (user action), rules (what happens), feedback (visual/audio response), loops and modes (repeating behavior)
Duration guidelines: 100-200ms simple transitions (opacity, color), 200-500ms complex transitions (expansion, movement), avoid >500ms for interactive elements
Easing curves: ease-out for entrances (decelerate into rest), ease-in for exits (accelerate out), ease-in-out for on-screen movement
Staggered animations: cascading reveal for lists with 50-75ms delay between items, total duration under 1 second
Functional vs decorative motion: functional serves usability (loading indicator), decorative serves personality (celebration confetti)
Motion accessibility: respecting prefers-reduced-motion system setting, providing alternatives for essential motion information
Motion specification: documenting property, duration, delay, easing curve, and trigger for developer handoff
lecture_notes.demonstration: Prototyping a card expansion micro-interaction in Figma with smart animate, specifying exact easing curves and durations in annotation layers for developer handoff
setup_requirements: After Effects or Principle (optional for advanced animation)

review.value: A contained product moment with four parts: trigger (user taps), rules (what happens mechanically), feedback (visual response confirming action), and loops/modes (ongoing state or repetition).
Mathematical curves controlling acceleration. Ease-out (decelerate) for entering elements -- objects in motion naturally slow to rest. Ease-in (accelerate) for exiting elements.
A CSS media query that detects when users have enabled reduced motion in OS settings. Designers must provide alternative static states for all animated content to respect this accessibility setting.
Like a performance budget, a motion budget limits total animation on screen at once. Too many simultaneous animations create visual noise that is worse than no animation at all.

learning_objectives: Design layouts that adapt across mobile, tablet, and desktop breakpoints
Implement WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards in design deliverables
Use auto-layout and constraints for responsive component behavior
Design for touch, mouse, and keyboard interaction paradigms simultaneously
readings: Responsive Web Design by Ethan Marcotte, Ch. 1-3
WCAG 2.1 Quick Reference (W3C)

lecture_notes.introduction: Over half of global web traffic comes from mobile devices. Responsive design is not an afterthought -- it is a foundational design constraint that must inform every decision from the earliest concept. A design that works beautifully on desktop but is unusable on mobile has failed the majority of its audience. Today we master responsive design: how to create layouts that adapt intelligently across screen sizes, how to handle touch targets that are large enough for finger interaction, how to manage content reflow when columns collapse, and how to maintain visual hierarchy when screen real estate shrinks dramatically. We also integrate accessibility throughout: contrast ratios, semantic heading structure, focus states for keyboard navigation, and touch target sizing. Accessibility is not a separate concern to address at the end -- it is a design principle embedded in every decision.
key_concepts: Breakpoint strategy: mobile-first (design small, enhance for large) vs desktop-first, with common breakpoints at 375px, 768px, 1024px, 1440px
Fluid grids and flexible images: percentage-based widths and max-width constraints that scale between breakpoints
Touch target sizing: minimum 44x44 CSS pixels (WCAG), 48x48dp (Material Design), with adequate spacing between targets
Semantic structure: heading hierarchy (H1 -> H2 -> H3), landmark regions (nav, main, aside, footer), meaningful alt text
Focus states: visible focus indicators for keyboard navigation, focus order matching visual order, skip links for content access
Content priority: determining which content is essential on mobile vs supplementary on desktop, and how to handle the difference
Auto-layout in Figma: building components that resize, wrap, and reflow based on content and container size
Testing methodology: checking designs on actual devices, using screen reader for accessibility audit, testing keyboard navigation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Converting a desktop Figma layout to tablet and mobile using auto-layout, constraints, and component variants, then running an accessibility audit checklist
setup_requirements: Screen reader (VoiceOver on macOS) for testing
Device preview tools for responsive testing

review.value: Starting from the smallest screen ensures core content is always accessible. Adding complexity for larger screens (progressive enhancement) is easier than removing it for smaller screens (graceful degradation).
Web Content Accessibility Guidelines: perceivable, operable, understandable, robust. AA is the legal baseline in many jurisdictions including the EU (EN 301 549) and US (Section 508).
Keyboard users navigate with Tab and Shift+Tab. Focus must follow a logical order matching visual layout. Interactive elements must have visible focus indicators that meet contrast requirements.
How content reorders when moving from multi-column desktop to single-column mobile. Sidebar content that is secondary on desktop may need to move below main content on mobile rather than above.

learning_objectives: Architect a scalable design system with atomic design methodology
Build reusable components with variants, states, and documentation
Establish design tokens that bridge design and development
Create documentation that enables other designers to use the system independently
readings: Atomic Design by Brad Frost, Ch. 2-4
Design Systems by Alla Kholmatova, Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Design systems are the single source of truth that keeps products visually and functionally consistent as they scale across teams, platforms, and time. A design system is not just a component library -- it is a set of standards, principles, and reusable elements that enable any team member to create consistent, high-quality design without starting from scratch. Today we architect a design system using atomic design methodology: building from the smallest elements (atoms: buttons, inputs, labels) through combinations (molecules: search bars, form fields) to complex structures (organisms: navigation headers, card grids) to page layouts (templates and pages). We define design tokens -- named variables for colors, spacing, typography, and motion -- that create a shared language between designers and developers. A design token named 'color-primary-500' in Figma maps directly to a CSS variable, eliminating translation errors and ensuring pixel-perfect implementation.
key_concepts: Atomic design hierarchy: atoms (button, input, label), molecules (search bar), organisms (nav header), templates (page layout), pages (real content)
Design tokens: named entities storing visual attributes (colors, spacing, typography, elevation, motion) as platform-agnostic variables
Component anatomy: base state, hover, focus, active, disabled, loading, error -- every interactive component needs all relevant states
Naming conventions: category/property/variant (color/primary/500, spacing/padding/large) for scalable organization
Documentation standards: usage guidelines, do/don't examples, code snippets, accessibility notes per component
Theming: light/dark mode through token swapping rather than redesigning components, maintaining contrast ratios in both themes
Contribution model: how team members propose new components, review process, deprecation policy
Design-to-development handoff: using design tokens, component specs, and interactive prototypes to minimize implementation ambiguity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a button component in Figma with 5 variants (primary, secondary, ghost, destructive, disabled), each with hover and focus states, linked to design tokens
setup_requirements: Figma with component library set up

review.value: Named entities storing visual design attributes as platform-agnostic variables. 'color-primary-500' in Figma = --color-primary-500 in CSS. Change once, update everywhere.
Brad Frost's methodology structuring UI in five levels: atoms (smallest elements), molecules (simple combinations), organisms (complex sections), templates (page structure), pages (real content).
Every interactive component needs: default, hover, focus (keyboard), active (pressed), disabled, loading, and error states. Missing states cause implementation gaps and accessibility failures.
Design systems have an initial cost but reduce design-to-development time by 30-50% at scale because teams reuse validated components instead of designing and building from scratch.

learning_objectives: Design icon sets with consistent style, weight, and optical alignment
Create illustration systems that extend brand identity with scalable visual language
Apply icon and illustration effectively in UI contexts for clarity and personality
Ensure iconographic accessibility with labels, tooltips, and alternative text
readings: The Icon Handbook by Jon Hicks, Ch. 3-5
Google Material Icons Design Guidelines

lecture_notes.introduction: Icons and illustrations are the visual shorthand of digital design. A well-designed icon communicates meaning instantly across language barriers. A cohesive illustration system extends brand personality into every corner of the product. Today we design both with the rigor and consistency they deserve. Icon design requires precision: every icon in a set must share the same visual weight, stroke width, corner radius, padding, and grid alignment. A mismatched icon is like a typo -- it breaks the visual rhythm and erodes trust in the design system. Illustration systems require strategic thinking: defining a visual language (flat vs dimensional, geometric vs organic, muted vs saturated) that reinforces brand personality and scales from small spot illustrations to full-page hero images. We also confront the accessibility reality: icons without labels are meaningless to screen reader users and ambiguous for sighted users unfamiliar with the metaphor. Every icon needs either a visible label, a tooltip, or appropriate ARIA attributes.
key_concepts: Icon grid: standardized canvas size (24x24px typical), padding zones, keyline shapes (circle, square, rectangle) for optical consistency
Stroke vs fill icons: outlined icons for toolbars and navigation, filled icons for selected/active states, maintaining consistent weight
Optical alignment: adjusting icon position within the grid so they appear centered even when mathematically off-center (triangles, circles)
Icon metaphor: choosing universally understood symbols (gear for settings, magnifying glass for search) vs culturally specific ones (mailbox varies globally)
Illustration system: defined color palette, character style, perspective rules, complexity levels, and application guidelines
Spot illustrations: small illustrations for empty states, error pages, onboarding, and feature highlights that add personality
Icon accessibility: visible labels for critical actions, aria-label for icon-only buttons, tooltip on hover/focus
SVG optimization: clean paths, minimal points, proper viewBox, and removing unnecessary metadata for performance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a 6-icon set on a 24px grid in Figma: creating consistent stroke weight, corner radius, and optical alignment, then exporting as optimized SVG
setup_requirements: Icon reference sheet with common metaphors

review.value: Mathematical center and visual center differ for non-square shapes. A play triangle must be shifted right of mathematical center to appear visually centered because the visual weight is on the left.
All icons in a set must share: stroke width (2px), corner radius (2px), padding (2px from edge), and visual weight. Inconsistency between icons makes the interface feel unpolished.

An icon without a label relies on the user recognizing the metaphor. Research shows icon recognition rates vary from 95% (search magnifying glass) to below 50% (hamburger menu). Always provide accessible labels.
A defined visual language for illustrations: color palette subset, character proportions, perspective rules, and complexity levels. Ensures all illustrations feel like they belong to the same family.

learning_objectives: Select appropriate chart types for different data relationships
Design clear, accurate, and accessible data visualizations
Apply visual encoding principles for effective data communication
Avoid common visualization mistakes that mislead or confuse
readings: Storytelling with Data by Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic, Ch. 2-4
The Visual Display of Quantitative Information by Tufte, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Data visualization translates numbers into visual patterns that humans can understand intuitively. A well-designed chart reveals insights that would be invisible in a spreadsheet. A poorly designed chart obscures information, misleads viewers, or simply confuses them. As digital designers, you will be asked to design dashboards, reports, and analytics interfaces that present data to users who must make decisions based on what they see. The stakes are real: a misleading chart in a medical dashboard could affect treatment decisions, a confusing analytics graph could lead to wrong business decisions. Today we learn the principles of effective data visualization: choosing the right chart type for the data relationship (comparison, composition, distribution, relationship), using visual encoding correctly (position is most accurate, area is least), maintaining data integrity (never truncating bar chart axes), and designing for accessibility (not relying on color alone to distinguish data series).
key_concepts: Chart type selection: bar (comparison), line (trend over time), scatter (correlation), pie (composition -- rarely the right choice), heatmap (density/matrix)
Visual encoding hierarchy: position (most accurate) > length > angle > area > color saturation > color hue (least accurate for quantitative data)
Data-ink ratio: maximizing the proportion of ink devoted to data vs decoration (remove gridlines, borders, 3D effects)
Accessibility in visualization: not relying solely on color (add patterns, labels), sufficient contrast, screen reader alternatives
Common mistakes: truncated y-axes on bar charts, dual y-axes (confusing), pie charts with too many slices, 3D effects distorting perception
Dashboard layout: key metrics at top (KPI cards), supporting charts below, progressive detail through drill-down interaction
Color in data viz: sequential palettes for ordered data, diverging for positive/negative, categorical for distinct groups (max 7-8 colors)
Annotation: labeling data points, trend lines, and notable events directly on the chart rather than relying on legends
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesigning a poorly designed dashboard: replacing misleading charts, fixing accessibility issues, and applying data-ink ratio principles to create a clear, honest visualization
setup_requirements: Color palette tools for data visualization

review.value: Humans perceive position differences (bar height) with 90%+ accuracy but area differences (bubble size) with only 50-70% accuracy. Use bar charts instead of bubble charts when precise comparison matters.

Starting a bar chart y-axis at non-zero exaggerates differences because bars encode data as length. A change from 48% to 52% looks trivial at 0-100% scale but dramatic at 45-55%. Always start bar charts at zero.
Edward Tufte's principle: maximize the share of ink that represents data. Remove chart borders, unnecessary gridlines, 3D effects, and decorative elements that add no information value.
8% of men and 0.5% of women have some form of color vision deficiency. Never use red/green as the only differentiator. Supplement color with patterns, shapes, or direct labels.

class_schedule.topic: Design for Dark Mode and Theming
learning_objectives: Design effective dark mode interfaces that maintain readability and hierarchy
Implement theming through design tokens for systematic light/dark switching
Address the unique accessibility challenges of dark interfaces
Create theme-aware components that adapt properly across color schemes
readings: Material Design Dark Theme Guidelines (Google)
Apple Human Interface Guidelines: Dark Mode

lecture_notes.introduction: Dark mode has evolved from a developer preference to a mainstream user expectation. Over 80% of smartphone users enable dark mode at least some of the time, and most major platforms now require apps to support both light and dark themes. But dark mode is not simply inverting colors -- it requires careful redesign of elevation systems, contrast ratios, color saturation, and imagery treatment. A naive color inversion produces harsh, unreadable interfaces where shadows become nonsensical glows and carefully calibrated contrast relationships break. Today we learn to design dark mode properly: using elevated surfaces instead of shadows for depth, reducing color saturation to prevent eye strain on dark backgrounds, ensuring text contrast meets accessibility standards in both themes, and building theme-aware components through design tokens that swap values systematically.
key_concepts: Dark mode elevation: using progressively lighter surface colors instead of shadows to communicate depth hierarchy
Color adjustment: reducing saturation by 20-30% for dark mode to prevent vibrant colors from being harsh against dark backgrounds
Contrast in dark mode: maintaining WCAG AA ratios while avoiding pure white (#FFFFFF) on pure black (#000000) which causes halation
Design token theming: defining semantic tokens (color-surface-primary, color-text-primary) that map to different values per theme
Image treatment: reducing image brightness/contrast in dark mode, adding dark overlays, handling transparent PNGs
Elevation system: Material Design's overlay system where higher elevation = lighter surface color in dark mode
User preference respect: following system theme setting, providing manual override, persisting user choice
Testing both themes: verifying every screen in both light and dark mode -- components that look perfect in light may be invisible in dark
lecture_notes.demonstration: Converting a light-theme Figma design to dark mode using token swapping: adjusting surface colors, text contrast, elevation system, and image treatment while maintaining visual hierarchy
setup_requirements: Figma with design token system from previous weeks
Light and dark theme token sets

review.value: Pure white text (#FFFFFF) on pure black (#000000) causes a glow effect that reduces readability. Use off-white (#E0E0E0) on dark gray (#121212) for comfortable reading in dark mode.
In light mode, shadows communicate depth. In dark mode, shadows are invisible. Instead, higher-elevation surfaces use progressively lighter background colors to suggest proximity to the viewer.
Using semantic token names like 'color-surface-primary' instead of literal values like '#FFFFFF' allows the same component to use white in light mode and dark gray in dark mode through token mapping.
Saturated colors (brand blue at 100% saturation) are comfortable on white backgrounds but harsh on dark backgrounds. Reduce saturation by 20-30% for the dark theme palette.

learning_objectives: Create interactive prototypes that communicate design intent to stakeholders and developers
Design interaction patterns for common user scenarios: onboarding, navigation, data entry, feedback
Use prototyping tools to validate design decisions through user testing
Specify interaction behaviors with enough detail for accurate implementation
readings: About Face by Alan Cooper, Ch. 10-12
Figma Prototyping Documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Static mockups cannot communicate how a design feels. Interaction -- the dynamic behavior of the interface as users engage with it -- determines whether a design is intuitive or frustrating. A prototyping communicates timing, transitions, feedback, and flow in ways that static screens and written specifications cannot. Today we build interactive prototypes that serve two audiences: stakeholders who need to experience the design before approving it, and developers who need to understand the intended behavior before implementing it. We use Figma's prototyping capabilities to create flows that demonstrate navigation patterns, form interactions, loading states, error handling, and micro-animations. The goal is not to build a functional app -- it is to create a convincing simulation that validates design decisions and communicates intent unambiguously.
key_concepts: Prototype fidelity spectrum: paper sketches (concept validation), wireframe clickthroughs (flow validation), high-fidelity interactive (visual + interaction validation)
Interaction patterns: onboarding flows, navigation transitions, form interactions, loading/success/error states, pull-to-refresh, swipe gestures
Figma prototyping: connections, interactions (click, hover, drag), animations (smart animate, dissolve, slide), overlays, scrolling regions
Prototype testing: task-based testing with prototypes, thinking-aloud protocol, identifying friction points before development
Interaction specification: documenting trigger, action, animation type, duration, easing, and target state for developer handoff
Edge cases: what happens when data is loading, when the list is empty, when the network fails, when content is very long or very short
Gesture design: swipe, long-press, pinch, double-tap conventions by platform, and when to use each
Prototyping limitations: prototypes simulate behavior but cannot validate performance, data handling, or edge case robustness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete onboarding flow prototype in Figma: 4 screens with animated transitions, interactive form fields, validation feedback, and branching paths based on user selections
setup_requirements: Figma with completed design screens from previous weeks
User testing task list for prototype validation

review.value: Match fidelity to the question you are answering. Testing navigation structure? Use wireframes. Testing visual appeal? Use high-fidelity. Testing interaction timing? Use animated prototypes. Never over-invest.
Figma's feature that automatically animates between matching layers across frames. Objects with the same layer name transition smoothly, enabling complex animations without manual keyframing.
The difference between a good design and a great design is edge case handling: empty states, error recovery, loading states, extremely long content, and zero-data scenarios.

Give users tasks ('Sign up for an account'), not instructions ('Click the blue button'). Observe where they hesitate, tap incorrectly, or express confusion. These friction points reveal design problems.

learning_objectives: Give and receive design critique using structured frameworks
Distinguish between subjective preference and objective design evaluation
Facilitate productive design reviews that improve work without demoralizing creators
Build a personal practice of seeking and incorporating feedback into iterative design
readings: Discussing Design by Adam Connor and Aaron Irizarry, Ch. 1-4
Critique Framework Guide (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Design critique is the most important collaborative skill a designer can develop. The ability to give specific, actionable feedback that improves work -- and to receive feedback without defensiveness -- determines how quickly you grow as a designer and how effectively you collaborate with teams. Most design feedback is useless: 'I like it,' 'I don't like it,' 'Make the logo bigger,' 'Can we try it in blue?' These are preference statements masquerading as critique. Real critique evaluates design decisions against stated objectives: 'The call-to-action button has insufficient contrast against the background (2.8:1 vs required 4.5:1), which will cause accessibility failures and likely reduce click-through rates.' This feedback is specific, objective, tied to a principle, and actionable. Today we practice both giving and receiving critique using structured frameworks. We learn to separate the work from the creator, focus on objectives rather than preferences, and facilitate group reviews that produce improvement rather than discouragement.
key_concepts: Critique vs criticism: critique evaluates decisions against objectives; criticism judges the creator. 'This doesn't work because...' vs 'You didn't do a good job.'
Structured critique framework: identify the objective, describe the design decision, evaluate against the objective, suggest alternatives if appropriate
Giving feedback: be specific (point to the element), be objective (reference principles, not preference), be actionable (suggest a direction, not just a problem)
Receiving feedback: listen without defending, ask clarifying questions, separate useful critique from noise, decide what to act on based on project goals
Facilitating group critique: set ground rules, assign roles (presenter, timekeeper, note-taker), focus discussion on objectives, end with clear action items
Liz Lerman's Critical Response Process: four steps from meaning-making to neutral questions to opinion with permission to discussion
Design review cadence: informal daily check-ins, weekly team reviews, milestone stakeholder presentations -- each with different depth and format
Building a feedback habit: seeking critique early and often, maintaining a revision log, tracking which feedback improved the work
lecture_notes.demonstration: Structured design critique session: each student presents a design, receives critique using the framework, and identifies the three most actionable pieces of feedback with specific revision plans
setup_requirements: Design work from previous assignments for critique

review.value: 'The button needs more contrast' is vague. 'The primary CTA button has 2.8:1 contrast against the background, below the WCAG AA requirement of 4.5:1. Increasing button color saturation to #1A73E8 would achieve 5.2:1.' That is specific.

Effective critique references the design's stated objectives: 'The goal is to increase sign-up conversion. This form has 12 fields, which research shows reduces conversion by 40%. Can we reduce to 5 essential fields?'

When receiving critique, notice the urge to explain or defend. Instead, ask: 'Can you be more specific about what's not working?' The most growth comes from feedback that initially makes you uncomfortable.
The earlier and more often you seek feedback, the smaller each revision becomes. Designers who wait until the design is 'finished' face the largest and most painful revisions.

learning_objectives: Create design specifications that developers can implement accurately without ambiguity
Structure Figma files for efficient developer inspection and asset export
Communicate design intent through annotations, specifications, and interactive prototypes
Build collaborative workflows that reduce friction between design and development teams
readings: Design Engineering Collaboration Guide (provided)
Figma Developer Handoff Documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: The most beautiful design is worthless if developers cannot implement it accurately. Design handoff -- the process of communicating design decisions to developers -- is where many projects lose quality. Spacing changes by a few pixels, colors shift slightly, animations are simplified or skipped, and edge cases are handled with developer guesses instead of designer intent. The gap between design and implementation is not a developer problem -- it is a communication problem that designers are responsible for solving. Today we learn to create design deliverables that eliminate ambiguity: structured Figma files with consistent naming, organized pages, and properly configured components; design tokens exported in developer-consumable formats; interaction specifications with exact timing and easing values; and comprehensive annotations covering spacing, alignment, responsive behavior, and edge cases. We also discuss the collaborative workflows that keep designers and developers aligned throughout implementation, not just at handoff.
key_concepts: Figma file organization: page structure (Cover, Components, Screens by flow, Prototypes), naming conventions, version history management
Design token export: Figma tokens plugin exporting to JSON/CSS custom properties for direct developer consumption
Specification annotations: spacing values, font specs, color tokens, border radius, elevation, opacity -- everything needed to implement without guessing
Responsive specifications: breakpoint-specific layouts with clear rules for how elements resize, reflow, and adapt
Asset export: configuring export settings for images (PNG/WebP with @1x/@2x/@3x), icons (SVG), and animations (Lottie)
Interaction specifications: documenting trigger, property, value, duration, easing curve, delay for every animated behavior
Developer inspection mode: teaching developers to use Figma's inspect panel for measurements, colors, and CSS values
Collaboration cadence: design-dev pairing sessions, implementation reviews, QA checks against design specs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Preparing a complete design handoff package in Figma: organizing screens, adding specification annotations, exporting tokens, preparing assets, and writing implementation notes
setup_requirements: Figma with completed design project from course work

review.value: The Figma file IS the specification. Developers should inspect it directly rather than relying on separate documents that become outdated. Keep the Figma file current as the authoritative reference.

Hand off design tokens (named values) rather than raw numbers. '--spacing-md: 16px' is more useful than '16px' because the developer knows to use the token, maintaining consistency if the value changes.
The screens you did not design are the ones developers have to guess. Document empty states, error states, loading states, very long content, and zero-data scenarios explicitly.
Schedule regular design-dev pairing sessions during implementation to catch misinterpretations early. A 15-minute review mid-implementation prevents a 2-hour rework after completion.

learning_objectives: Build a design portfolio that demonstrates process, principles, and professional readiness
Present design work effectively in portfolio reviews and interviews
Navigate the digital design career landscape across product, brand, UX, and visual design roles
Develop a personal design philosophy that guides career decisions
readings: Design Portfolio Best Practices (provided compilation)
Career Paths in Digital Design (provided reading)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your design portfolio is a design project itself. It must demonstrate the same principles you learned in this course: clear hierarchy, intentional typography, accessible color, responsive layout, and purposeful motion. But beyond visual quality, it must tell the story of how you think. Hiring managers are not looking for pixel-perfect mockups -- they are looking for evidence that you can identify problems, explore solutions, make decisions with reasoning, and collaborate with teams. Today we focus on portfolio construction as a strategic communication exercise. We structure case studies that walk through your design process from research through iteration to final design, showing the thinking behind the pixels. We practice the portfolio walkthrough: a 10-15 minute guided tour through your strongest work that you will deliver in every design interview. And we discuss the design career landscape: product designer, visual designer, brand designer, UX designer, design system designer, and the skills each role emphasizes.
key_concepts: Portfolio as design: apply course principles to your own portfolio -- grid, type, color, hierarchy, responsiveness
Case study structure: context/problem, research/discovery, exploration/iteration, solution/final design, results/impact, reflection
Portfolio walkthrough: 10-15 minute guided presentation of 2-3 strongest projects, practicing narrative flow and anticipating questions
Career paths: product design (UI+UX), visual design (brand+marketing), UX design (research+strategy), design systems (components+tokens)
Portfolio platforms: personal website (most professional), Behance (discovery), Dribbble (visual showcase), each with different audiences
Common portfolio mistakes: showing only final designs without process, no role clarification on team projects, outdated work, broken responsive layout
Design philosophy: articulating your principles (accessibility-first, user-centered, data-informed) to guide career decisions
Interview preparation: whiteboard challenges, redesign exercises, cultural fit conversations, portfolio critique response
lecture_notes.demonstration: Portfolio review workshop: each student presents one case study, receives structured feedback on narrative, visual presentation, and persuasiveness
setup_requirements: Portfolio website with at least 2 case studies in progress

review.value: Hiring managers value evidence of design thinking more than pixel-perfect deliverables. Show your research, sketches, iterations, and reasoning alongside final designs.
For team projects, clearly state YOUR contribution. 'I designed the component library and led the design system architecture' is much more useful than 'I worked on the design team.'
Practice your walkthrough until it feels natural, not rehearsed. Anticipate questions: 'Why did you choose that approach?' 'What would you do differently?' 'How did you measure success?'
'I believe accessible design is not a constraint but a creative catalyst that produces better solutions for everyone.' A clear philosophy helps you evaluate opportunities and communicate your value.

class_schedule.topic: Final Portfolio Presentations and Course Synthesis
learning_objectives: Present a completed design portfolio demonstrating mastery of digital design principles
Provide constructive critique of peer portfolios using course frameworks
Synthesize fifteen weeks of learning into a personal design practice
Connect course skills to emerging design trends and career opportunities
readings: Review all course materials for synthesis
Peer portfolios for critique preparation

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session is your design debut. Each student presents their portfolio to the class in a format that mirrors industry portfolio reviews: a structured walkthrough of your strongest work, followed by Q&A from peers and the instructor. Beyond the presentations, we synthesize fifteen weeks of learning into personal design practices. The principles you have learned -- grid systems, typographic hierarchy, color accessibility, responsive design, motion purpose, design systems, and critique -- are not just academic concepts. They are the daily tools of professional digital designers. Every time you open Figma for the rest of your career, these principles will guide your decisions. The design field is evolving rapidly: AI tools are changing how designers prototype and iterate, variable fonts and container queries are expanding responsive design possibilities, spatial computing is creating new interaction paradigms, and the demand for accessible, inclusive design is growing as regulation catches up with best practice. The technical foundations you have built in this course will serve as the base for engaging with these emerging capabilities.
key_concepts: Professional presentation: structured portfolio walkthrough with confident narrative, handling questions gracefully
Peer critique: specific, principle-based feedback that helps classmates improve their portfolios before job applications
Design trends: AI-assisted design tools, variable fonts, container queries, spatial computing UI, inclusive design maturity
Personal design practice: continuous learning habits, critique-seeking behavior, staying current with design evolution
Design community: Figma Community, Dribbble, design conferences (Config, Clarity, An Event Apart), local meetups
Continuous skill development: new tools (Framer, Webflow), emerging platforms (visionOS, spatial computing), design leadership skills
Portfolio maintenance: quarterly updates, removing weaker pieces, adding new work, keeping technology current
Professional identity: defining your design point of view, what kind of problems you want to solve, and how you approach design differently
lecture_notes.demonstration: Student portfolio presentations with structured peer critique, followed by group synthesis discussion on design principles and career planning
setup_requirements: Final portfolio URL for peer review

review.value: Your portfolio, your skills, and your design philosophy should evolve continuously. The designer you are today should be noticeably better than the designer you were fifteen weeks ago.
When stakeholders ask 'Can we make it pop?' you now have the vocabulary and framework to translate that into specific, principled design decisions rather than arbitrary changes.
Accessible design is not a checkbox to verify at the end. It is a design principle embedded in every decision from color selection to component architecture to motion specification.
The ability to give and receive design critique is the single skill that most accelerates designer growth. Seek it early, seek it often, and always prefer honest feedback over comfortable agreement.

quizzes.topic: Layout Systems, Typography, Branding, and Color

questions.question: A designer is creating a responsive web layout and needs to choose between a 12-column grid and a modular grid. Explain when each is appropriate, and describe how a 12-column grid adapts across three breakpoints (mobile, tablet, desktop).
questions.answer: 12-column grid: ideal for content-heavy layouts (dashboards, editorial) because columns group flexibly (1,2,3,4,6,12). Modular grid: better for image/card-heavy layouts needing both horizontal and vertical alignment. Breakpoints: mobile = 4 columns with 16px margins, tablet = 8 columns with 24px margins, desktop = 12 columns with 24-32px margins. Gutters may adjust at each breakpoint.

rubric.key: Correct use cases for each grid type

questions.question: What is the minimum contrast ratio for normal text under WCAG 2.1 AA, and why does this standard matter?
questions.answer: 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text (18pt+/14pt bold). Matters because ~8% of men have color vision deficiency, low contrast affects users in bright environments and on low-quality screens, and it is a legal requirement under accessibility laws (Section 508, EN 301 549).

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'clear space' in brand identity design and why it matters for brand recognition across platforms.
questions.answer: Clear space is the minimum protected area around a logo where no other elements may intrude, typically defined as a multiple of a logo unit. It ensures the logo is always visually prominent and recognizable, never crowded by surrounding content. Matters across platforms because a logo without clear space on a busy social media post, a cramped app icon, or a cluttered business card loses its visual impact and becomes harder to recognize, weakening brand identity.

rubric.key: Connects to brand recognition across platforms

questions.question: A mobile app displays a list of 8 cards that animate into view. Specify duration, easing curve, and stagger delay values, and explain why each choice improves UX.
questions.answer: 200-300ms per card with ease-out (cubic-bezier(0.0, 0.0, 0.2, 1.0)). Stagger: 50-75ms between items, total ~550-825ms. Ease-out: entering elements naturally decelerate to rest. Stagger creates visual cascade showing ordered group. Total under 1 second prevents sluggish perception.

questions.question: Explain the atomic design methodology and how design tokens fit into a design system. Give a concrete token example in both Figma and CSS.
questions.answer: Atomic design: atoms (buttons, labels), molecules (search bar), organisms (nav header), templates (page layout), pages (real content). Tokens store visual attributes as named variables. Example: Figma color style 'color/primary/500' = #2563EB; CSS: --color-primary-500: #2563EB; used as background-color: var(--color-primary-500). Changing the token propagates everywhere.

questions.question: What is the prefers-reduced-motion CSS media query, and how should designers account for it in their motion specifications?
questions.answer: A media query detecting when users enable reduced motion in OS accessibility settings (for motion sensitivity, vestibular disorders, or preference). Designers must: (1) provide static alternatives for all animated content, (2) annotate which animations are essential (should play with reduced duration) vs decorative (should be removed), (3) ensure no information is communicated solely through motion.

rubric.key: Correct definition of the media query
Specific design guidance for handling it

quizzes.topic: Responsive Design, Design Systems, and Iconography

questions.question: Explain mobile-first responsive design and give two specific advantages over desktop-first approach.
questions.answer: Mobile-first: design for smallest screen first, then progressively enhance for larger screens. Advantage 1: forces content prioritization -- with limited space, you must identify what is truly essential, producing cleaner designs at all sizes. Advantage 2: progressive enhancement is technically simpler than graceful degradation -- adding features for capable devices is more reliable than removing them for constrained devices.

questions.question: An icon set has 20 icons but three of them look noticeably heavier/thicker than the rest. What design properties need to be consistent across all icons, and how would you fix the inconsistency?
questions.answer: Consistent properties: stroke width (e.g., all 2px), corner radius (all 2px rounded), padding from grid edge (all 2px inset), overall visual weight (similar fill-to-empty ratio). Fix: overlay the inconsistent icons on the consistent ones using the same grid. Identify which properties differ -- likely the stroke width is thicker (2.5px vs 2px) or the icon fills more of the grid space. Redraw the three icons matching the exact specifications of the other 17.

questions.question: What is the difference between a component variant and a component state in a design system? Give an example of each for a button component.
questions.answer: Variant: a fundamentally different visual style of the component serving different purposes. Button variants: primary (filled, main action), secondary (outlined, secondary action), ghost (text-only, tertiary action), destructive (red, dangerous action). State: the visual appearance at a specific moment of interaction. Button states: default (resting), hover (cursor over), focus (keyboard-selected), active (being pressed), disabled (non-interactive). Variants and states combine: a primary button in hover state, a destructive button in disabled state.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between variant and state

quizzes.topic: Data Visualization, Dark Mode, and Prototyping

questions.question: Why is a pie chart usually a poor choice for data visualization? Name the specific perceptual limitation and suggest a better alternative for comparing 5 categories.
questions.answer: Pie charts encode data as angles, which humans perceive with low accuracy (~50% precision vs 90%+ for position/length). Comparing similar-sized slices is nearly impossible, and pie charts with more than 3-4 slices become unreadable. Better alternative for 5 categories: horizontal bar chart. Bars encode data as length (high perceptual accuracy), enable direct comparison, accommodate labels easily, and scale to any number of categories.

questions.question: Explain the concept of halation in dark mode design and how to prevent it.
questions.answer: Halation is a glowing/bleeding effect that occurs when pure white (#FFFFFF) text is displayed on pure black (#000000) backgrounds. The high contrast causes the white to appear to bleed into surrounding dark areas, reducing readability and causing eye strain. Prevention: use off-white text (#E0E0E0 to #F5F5F5) on dark gray backgrounds (#121212 to #1E1E1E) instead of pure extremes. This reduces perceived halation while maintaining sufficient WCAG contrast ratios.

rubric.key: Correct cause (pure white on pure black)

questions.question: What is the difference between a low-fidelity wireframe prototype and a high-fidelity interactive prototype? When should you use each?
questions.answer: Low-fidelity wireframe: grayscale, minimal detail, basic shapes and placeholder text. Focuses on layout, flow, and information architecture. Use early in design process to validate structure and navigation before investing in visual design. High-fidelity interactive: full visual design with real content, animated transitions, and interactive components. Focuses on visual quality, motion, and detailed interaction behavior. Use later to validate the complete experience, test with users, and communicate final intent to developers.

quizzes.topic: Critique, Handoff, Portfolio, and Professional Practice

questions.question: Rewrite this vague design feedback as specific, actionable critique: 'The homepage feels cluttered and I don't know where to look.'
questions.answer: Specific rewrite: 'The homepage has 4 elements competing for primary visual hierarchy (hero banner, feature callout, news feed, and CTA button are all similarly sized and colored). The primary conversion goal (sign-up CTA) should be the clear focal point. Recommendation: increase CTA size and contrast, reduce the feature callout to a secondary visual weight, and add whitespace between sections to create clear visual grouping using the Gestalt principle of proximity.'

rubric.key: Identifies specific elements causing the problem
References a design principle or objective

questions.question: Name three things a designer should include in a design handoff package that are commonly forgotten, resulting in developer guesswork.
questions.answer: 1) Edge case states: empty states (no data), error states (network failure, validation errors), loading states (skeleton screens), and extremely long content behavior. These are the screens designers skip but developers must build. 2) Interaction specifications: exact animation duration, easing curve, delay, and trigger for every transition and micro-interaction. Without specs, developers either skip animations or implement them with default values. 3) Responsive behavior rules: how specific components adapt between breakpoints -- which elements stack, hide, resize, or reflow. Without rules, developers guess at intermediate screen sizes.

rubric.key: Explains why each causes developer problems

questions.question: What is the single most important difference between a junior designer portfolio and a senior designer portfolio?
questions.answer: The most important difference is evidence of decision-making and rationale. A junior portfolio shows what was designed (beautiful final screens). A senior portfolio shows why it was designed that way (research findings that informed decisions, trade-offs evaluated, alternatives explored and rejected with reasoning, measurable results of the design). Senior portfolios demonstrate strategic thinking and business impact, not just execution quality. The artifacts matter less than the thinking behind them.

rubric.key: Contrasts junior execution vs senior strategy

major_assignments.title: Brand Identity and Responsive Landing Page
major_assignments.description: Design a complete brand identity for a fictional product or service, then apply it to a responsive landing page at three breakpoints (mobile 375px, tablet 768px, desktop 1440px). Demonstrate mastery of grid systems, typography, color, accessibility, and brand consistency.
objectives: Create a cohesive brand identity system from scratch with professional documentation
Apply grid-based layout principles across responsive breakpoints with auto-layout
Demonstrate accessible color and typography choices meeting WCAG 2.1 AA
Deliver design artifacts ready for developer handoff with annotations
deliverables: Brand style guide (logo, color palette with hex values, type scale, spacing system, usage rules)
Figma file with responsive landing page at 3 breakpoints using auto-layout and constraints
Accessibility annotation layer showing contrast ratios, heading hierarchy, touch targets, and focus states
Written design rationale explaining decisions (3-4 pages)

criteria: Logo is clear, scalable, works in mono and color at all sizes
Color palette has verified WCAG AA contrast and includes accessible pairings
Typography selections appropriate and well-paired with documented scale
Style guide thorough with correct/incorrect usage examples
Grid system consistent and well-defined at each breakpoint
Content reflows logically across screen sizes
Auto-layout used correctly for flexible components
Spacing follows a defined scale
All text meets WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratios verified with tools
Touch targets meet 44x44px minimum
Heading hierarchy is semantic and logical
Focus states designed for all interactive elements
Written rationale clearly explains design decisions with principle references
Brand choices justified for target audience
Technical decisions documented (grid, scale, tokens)
Professional writing quality
Complete brand identity with professional deliverables
Responsive layout with strong grid and hierarchy
Accessibility standards met or exceeded
Clear documentation connecting decisions to principles

major_assignments.title: Design System Component Library
major_assignments.description: Build a functional design system in Figma containing minimum 12 components with full variant sets, design tokens, light/dark theme support, and comprehensive documentation. Include interactive prototypes demonstrating motion design and a developer handoff package.
objectives: Architect a scalable component library using atomic design principles
Define design tokens for color, typography, spacing, elevation, and motion
Create components with complete state coverage and light/dark theme variants
Produce documentation and developer handoff materials for production use
deliverables: Figma library with 12+ components organized by atomic level
Interactive prototype showing at least 4 micro-interactions with motion specs
Component documentation pages with usage guidelines, do/don't examples, and accessibility notes
Complete dark theme variant for all components with verified contrast
Developer handoff annotations with spacing, token references, and interaction specifications

criteria: All components have complete variant and state coverage (default, hover, focus, active, disabled, error)
Naming conventions consistent and scalable following atomic design hierarchy
Components use auto-layout and constraints correctly for responsive behavior
Nested components properly structured with clear override points
Token naming follows clear hierarchy (category/property/variant)
Tokens cover color, typography, spacing, elevation, border radius, and motion
Light and dark themes implemented via token swapping with verified contrast in both
Token documentation is complete and developer-consumable (JSON format)
At least 4 micro-interactions prototyped with specific motion specs
Duration and easing values specified and appropriate for each animation
Animations enhance usability with clear purpose (feedback, transition, attention)
prefers-reduced-motion alternatives documented for all animations
Each component has usage guidelines with context examples
Do/don't examples are clear, accurate, and instructive
Accessibility notes included per component (contrast, keyboard, screen reader)
Developer handoff annotations are comprehensive enough for accurate implementation
Comprehensive component library with professional organization and naming
Well-structured design tokens supporting theming across light and dark modes
Purposeful motion design with accessibility considerations
Documentation enabling independent use by other designers and developers

major_assignments.title: Complete Product Design Case Study
major_assignments.description: Design a complete multi-screen digital product (app or web application) from research through final design, documenting the process as a portfolio-ready case study. The project must demonstrate all course principles: layout, typography, color, accessibility, responsive design, motion, and design system thinking.
objectives: Execute a complete design process from problem definition through polished final design
Create a portfolio-ready case study that demonstrates design thinking and principle application
Build responsive, accessible, theme-aware designs with motion and interaction specification
Present work professionally in a format suitable for industry portfolio reviews
deliverables: Complete Figma design file with 8+ screens at mobile and desktop breakpoints
Portfolio case study document: problem, research, exploration, solution, principles applied (6-8 pages)
Interactive prototype demonstrating core user flow with motion design
Accessibility audit report with verified contrast ratios, heading structure, and keyboard navigation
Design system excerpt: 5+ reusable components with tokens, variants, and documentation

criteria: Layout demonstrates mastery of grid systems and visual hierarchy
Typography and color choices are intentional, accessible, and well-documented
Responsive design adapts intelligently across breakpoints
Overall aesthetic quality is professional and consistent
Case study clearly documents the design process from problem through solution
Research and exploration phases show genuine investigation, not post-hoc rationalization
Design decisions are justified with reference to course principles and user needs
Iteration is documented with before/after comparisons
Components are well-structured with proper variants, states, and tokens
Interactive prototype demonstrates intended interaction behavior
Accessibility audit identifies and addresses all major issues
Design system excerpt is production-ready quality
Case study is written for a portfolio audience (clear, concise, visually engaging)
Work would make a strong impression in a 10-minute portfolio walkthrough
Design philosophy and principles are evident throughout
Professional quality suitable for job applications
Complete product design demonstrating mastery of all course principles
Portfolio-ready case study with clear process documentation
Technical execution meeting professional accessibility and responsiveness standards
Presentation quality suitable for industry portfolio reviews

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes assessing design theory, terminology, accessibility standards, and critical analysis skills

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on Figma exercises applying layout, typography, color, motion, and design system principles

major_assignments.description: Three comprehensive projects: brand identity with responsive page, design system library, and product design case study

midterm_examination.description: Mixed format exam covering design principles, accessibility standards, and applied design analysis

final_project.description: Capstone product design case study demonstrating integrated mastery of all course principles

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all critique sessions and lab workshops. More than 2 unexcused absences result in 5% grade reduction per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work penalized 10% per day. Critique deadlines are firm because peer reviews depend on timely submissions.
course_policies.collaboration: Design critiques are collaborative. Individual assignments must be your own work. Inspiration sources and asset credits must be documented.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All design work must be original. Stock assets permitted only where explicitly allowed and must be credited. AI-generated designs submitted without disclosure is a violation.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a Figma account and reliable internet. Adobe Creative Cloud recommended but not required. Lab computers available with all software.
